summary: Do not ship this plan to issue slicing yet. Core lifecycle, conflict-resolution, and backup-recovery semantics remain undefined or contradictory.
triage: review-gate SKILL.md rules govern: render EVERY finding as a full table row, the human decides each row (accept/reject/defer/question — free text), echo back before applying, max 2 rounds, append decisions.md and COMMIT it with this artifact.

findings.title: Placeholder values change on unrelated recompilations
findings.body: The user story promises a new placeholder value when a Profile is enabled, but the plan substitutes placeholders on every Compile and recompiles on tab events and alarms. A tab navigation, tab closure, or timer can therefore silently rotate UUID/timestamp header values while the Profile remains enabled, breaking correlation and authentication scenarios. A competent implementation following these decisions would violate story 16.
findings.recommendation: Define placeholder materialization as persisted activation state: generate values only on disabled→enabled transitions, retain them across unrelated recompiles and service-worker restarts, and add lifecycle tests covering tab/alarm-triggered recompilation.

findings.title: Open question: conflicting active Profiles have no deterministic semantics
findings.body: The PRD supports multiple simultaneously active Profiles, but never defines precedence when they modify or redirect the same request. Profile ordering is presented only as a convenience, while DNR priority and conflict behavior are central to the compiled result. Implementers would have to invent whether conflicts follow profile order, modification order, action type, or rejection/warning rules, producing unpredictable traffic modification.
findings.recommendation: Specify deterministic conflict semantics for every action combination, including header override/append/remove, redirect, CSP, and Exclude allow rules. Define stable rule-priority allocation and observable warnings, then cover overlapping Profiles in compiler and browser tests.

findings.title: Recompile-and-replace lifecycle permits stale rule sets to win
findings.body: The plan triggers full recompilation from storage changes, startup, tab events, and alarms but gives no serialization, generation, or stale-result rejection rule. Concurrent asynchronous handlers can compile different snapshots and apply them out of order, allowing an older tab state or pre-Pause Profile state to overwrite the newest rules. That can leave traffic modification active after the user believes it has stopped.
findings.recommendation: Define a single reconciliation queue with monotonic generations or cancellation, specify how the latest storage and tab snapshot is obtained, and require stress tests where Pause/profile edits race tab and alarm events.

findings.title: Automatic snapshot backup has no bounded retention or partial-write recovery
findings.body: The plan proposes automatic, chunked snapshots in a quota-limited store but does not define retention, snapshot commit atomicity, cleanup, or restoration behavior for missing/corrupt chunks. Repeated backups will eventually exhaust the stated quota, and an interrupted multi-item write can leave a snapshot visible but unrestorable. Round-trip testing alone does not exercise these failure modes.
findings.recommendation: Specify a bounded retention policy, immutable snapshot IDs, manifest-last commit protocol, integrity metadata, cleanup after failed writes, and preservation of the last known-good snapshot. Test quota exhaustion and interrupted/corrupt chunk recovery.

next_steps: Resolve these lifecycle and conflict decisions in the PRD before slicing implementation issues.
Turn each resolved invariant into compiler/store tests plus concrete real-browser scenarios.
